name: Whydah Gally
story: A new London slave ship captured by Bellamy in the Windward Passage in February 1717 and made his flagship. She was driven ashore in a violent nor'easter off Wellfleet, Cape Cod, on 26 April 1717; about 144 men drowned and only two from the Whydah survived. Six captured survivors from the flotilla were tried in Boston and most were hanged.
cargo: Plunder from some 50 prizes: gold dust, Spanish and other coin, ivory, indigo; period claims of 4-5 tons of treasure.
discovery: Located in 1984 by Barry Clifford; identity proven by the ship's bell inscribed 'THE WHYDAH GALLY 1716' (raised 1985). The first authenticated pirate shipwreck ever found; artifacts at the Whydah Pirate Museum, West Yarmouth, MA.
sources: Whydah Pirate Museum / Barry Clifford, Expedition Whydah (1999)
Massachusetts Board of Underwater Archaeological Resources
Colin Woodard, The Republic of Pirates (2007)

name: Queen Anne's Revenge (ex-La Concorde)
ship_type: frigate-built slave ship, pirate flagship (~200 tons, up to 40 guns)
story: The Nantes slaver La Concorde was taken by Blackbeard off Martinique in November 1717 and renamed Queen Anne's Revenge. After blockading Charleston in May 1718, Blackbeard ran her aground on the bar at Beaufort (Topsail) Inlet, North Carolina, in June 1718 — possibly deliberately, to break up his oversized company. He escaped with the pick of the loot in the sloop Adventure.
cargo: Cannon (more than 30 recovered), gold dust traces, medical instruments (some marked from the French surgeons), everyday pirate material culture; no treasure hoard.
discovery: Found 21 November 1996 by Intersal Inc.; excavated since by the North Carolina QAR Project; identity confirmed and announced as QAR (state-confirmed 2011). Site lies ~1.5 km offshore in ~8.5 m of water.
sources: North Carolina Dept. of Natural and Cultural Resources, QAR Project (qaronline.org)
Mark Wilde-Ramsing & Linda Carnes-McNaughton, Blackbeard's Sunken Prize (2018)

name: Urca de Lima (Santisima Trinidad), 1715 Treasure Fleet
story: One of the combined New Spain flota and Tierra Firme squadron (11 of 12 ships lost) wrecked by a hurricane on the night of 30-31 July 1715 along Florida's east coast; over 1,000 men died fleet-wide. The Urca de Lima grounded relatively intact near Fort Pierce and served as a base for Spanish salvage camps.
cargo: Hides, cocoa, sassafras, incense and private silver; most registered treasure was salvaged by the Spanish 1715-1718.
discovery: Relocated in 1928; stripped by salvors; dedicated in 1987 as Florida's first Underwater Archaeological Preserve, off Pepper Park Beach, Fort Pierce.
sources: Florida Division of Historical Resources, Urca de Lima Underwater Archaeological Preserve (museumsinthesea.com)
Robert F. Marx, Shipwrecks in the Americas

name: Nuestra Senora de la Regla (Capitana of Ubilla), 1715 Treasure Fleet
ship_type: galleon, capitana of the New Spain flota
story: Flagship of the 1715 fleet, smashed in the 31 July 1715 hurricane just south of present-day Sebastian Inlet, Florida; Ubilla and over 200 aboard died. Spanish salvage camps on the beach were raided in 1716 by English privateer-turned-pirate Henry Jennings, an event that helped seed the Nassau pirate republic.
cargo: Roughly 1,300 chests of newly minted Mexican silver cobs plus gold; partially salvaged by Spain, with major modern recoveries of coins ('Cabin Wreck' site).
discovery: Modern relocation by Kip Wagner and the Real Eight Company c.1960-61, launching the Florida treasure-salvage era; the McLarty Treasure Museum stands at the Spanish salvors' camp site.
sources: Florida Division of Historical Resources / McLarty Treasure Museum
Kip Wagner, Pieces of Eight (1966)
Colin Woodard, The Republic of Pirates (2007)

name: San Pedro, 1733 New Spain Flota
story: Part of the New Spain flota of Lieutenant General Rodrigo de Torres that left Havana on 13 July 1733 and was wrecked along the Florida Keys by a hurricane on 15 July; most of the ~20 ships grounded or sank between Key Largo and Vaca Key. Spain mounted a highly effective salvage operation and recovered more silver than the manifests listed (exposing contraband).
cargo: Mexican silver coin (16,000 pesos registered), Chinese porcelain, cochineal, indigo, hides.
discovery: Relocated in 1960 in Hawk Channel south of Indian Key in ~5.5 m of water; designated the San Pedro Underwater Archaeological Preserve State Park in 1989.
sources: Florida Division of Historical Resources, San Pedro Underwater Archaeological Preserve
Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary 1733 Spanish Galleon Trail

name: El Rubi Segundo (Capitana), 1733 New Spain Flota
ship_type: galleon / warship, capitana of the flota
captain: Rodrigo de Torres (lieutenant general of the fleet)
story: Flagship of the 1733 flota, driven onto the reefs off the upper Keys in the 15 July 1733 hurricane. Survivors camped on the islands while Spanish divers salvaged the registered treasure; the wrecks were then burned to the waterline to deter freelance wreckers.
cargo: Silver specie and bullion (largely recovered by Spanish salvors 1733-34), plus general New Spain produce.
discovery: Found in 1938 off Plantation Key by pioneering hard-hat salvor Art McKee, who built his 'Museum of Sunken Treasure' around it — often called the first treasure-wreck salvage in the Keys.
sources: Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary, 1733 Spanish Galleon Trail (Capitana El Rubi)
Robert F. Marx, Shipwrecks in the Americas

name: San Jose
ship_type: 60-gun galleon, capitana of the Tierra Firme galeones
captain: Jose Fernandez de Santillan, Conde de Casa Alegre (killed)
story: Flagship of the Tierra Firme fleet carrying Peruvian treasure from Portobelo toward Cartagena and Havana during the War of the Spanish Succession. On 8 June 1708 she fought Commodore Charles Wager's squadron in Wager's Action off Baru and blew up; only 11 of nearly 600 men survived. Often called the 'holy grail of shipwrecks'.
cargo: Registered gold and silver coin and bullion plus emeralds, commonly estimated at 7-11 million pesos; modern valuations in the billions of dollars.
discovery: Located 27 November 2015 by the Colombian government with Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution's REMUS 6000 AUV at ~600 m depth off the Baru peninsula; exact coordinates are a Colombian state secret and the wreck remains the subject of litigation (Sea Search Armada) and Spain/Colombia/Bolivian Qhara Qhara claims.
sources: Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution press release (2018) on the REMUS 6000 find
Carla Rahn Phillips, The Treasure of the San Jose (2007)
Government of Colombia / ICANH statements 2015-2024

name: Adventure Galley
story: Purpose-built in Deptford in 1695 for Kidd's pirate-hunting/privateering venture backed by Whig grandees. After taking the Quedagh Merchant, Kidd reached the pirate haven of Ile Sainte-Marie, Madagascar, in April 1698, where the leaky, worm-eaten galley was stripped, run aground and burned/scuttled in the harbor.
cargo: Stripped before loss; fittings and ballast only.
discovery: Hull remains in the shallow harbor have never been conclusively identified. A 2015 claim by Barry Clifford to have found the wreck and a 50 kg 'silver ingot' was investigated by a UNESCO expert mission, which found the 'ingot' was lead ballast and the supposed wreck likely part of old port works — a well-documented debunking.
sources: UNESCO expert mission report on Ile Sainte-Marie (2015)
Robert C. Ritchie, Captain Kidd and the War against the Pirates (1986)

name: Quedagh Merchant (renamed Adventure Prize)
nationality: Mughal-Indian owned (Armenian charter, French passes); prize of William Kidd
story: The rich Indian merchantman Kidd took off the Malabar coast on 30 January 1698 — the capture that made him a wanted pirate. Sailing to the Caribbean in 1699, Kidd left her at Isla Catalina off Hispaniola while he went north to negotiate a pardon; the men left behind looted and burned her and she sank in shallow water.
cargo: Originally silks, muslins, sugar, opium, iron and gold; mostly sold or plundered before the burning. Anchors and 26 cannon remained on site.
discovery: Found in December 2007 in ~3 m of water 20 m off Isla Catalina, Dominican Republic; investigated by Indiana University and dedicated in 2011 as a 'Living Museum of the Sea' underwater preserve.
sources: Indiana University news releases 2007-2011 (Charles Beeker, Office of Underwater Science)
Robert C. Ritchie, Captain Kidd and the War against the Pirates (1986)

name: Fiery Dragon (ex-Flying Dragon)
captain: Christopher Condent (William Condon); ship burned after his retirement
story: Condent took the Fiery Dragon on the Pirate Round and in 1720 captured a Mughal pilgrim ship off Bombay worth a reported 150,000 pounds, then retired to Ile Sainte-Marie and negotiated a French pardon (settling at Bourbon). The ship burned and sank in Sainte-Marie's harbor in 1721.
cargo: Remnants of East India plunder: Kangxi-period Chinese export porcelain, Indian and Arabian gold, glass and organic trade goods found in the wreck deposit.
discovery: Investigated in 1999-2000s by expeditions associated with Barry Clifford and historian John de Bry, who identified the burned wreck in the Baie des Forbans as the Fiery Dragon; identification is credible but not universally endorsed (the same harbor confusion criticized by the 2015 UNESCO mission).
sources: John de Bry / Center for Historical Archaeology reports on Ile Sainte-Marie
UNESCO expert mission report (2015)
E.T. Fox & period depositions on Condent

name: Speaker
story: A slave ship seized by pirates off Madagascar in 1700 and commanded by John Bowen on the Pirate Round. Returning from a successful Red Sea/India cruise, she struck the barrier reef on the east coast of Mauritius on 7 January 1702; the crew got ashore, were hospitably received by the Dutch, and bought a sloop to leave. One of the earliest pirate wrecks ever excavated.
cargo: Red Sea and Indian Ocean plunder: gold and silver coin (including Arabian and Mughal issues), some recovered in excavation.
discovery: Found 1979-1980 off the east coast of Mauritius near Grande Riviere Sud-Est by Patrick Lize and Jacques Dumas; finds published and partly displayed in Mauritius.
sources: Patrick Lize, 'The Wreck of the Pirate Ship Speaker on Mauritius in 1702', IJNA 13 (1984)
Charles Johnson, A General History of the Pyrates (1724)

name: Henrietta Marie
story: A London merchant slaver that delivered about 190 enslaved Africans at Port Royal, Jamaica, in May 1700 and sank in a storm on New Ground Reef ~35 miles west of Key West on the homeward leg; all hands were lost. It is the earliest slave-ship wreck identified by name in the Americas and a touchstone for African-American maritime memory.
cargo: Homeward cargo of sugar, indigo, cotton and dyewood, plus more than 80 sets of iron shackles (bilboes), trade beads and pewter from the unsold trade goods.
discovery: Found in 1972 by divers working for Mel Fisher; identified in 1983 when the ship's bell inscribed 'The Henrietta Marie 1699' was recovered; memorialized underwater by the National Association of Black Scuba Divers (1993).
sources: Mel Fisher Maritime Museum, 'A Slave Ship Speaks: The Wreck of the Henrietta Marie'
Madeleine Burnside & Rosemarie Robotham, Spirits of the Passage (1997)

name: 't Vliegend Hert (Flying Hart)
story: Outward bound from Rammekens for Batavia on 3 February 1735, the Vliegend Hert and the smaller Anna Catharina both struck in the Deurloo/Schooneveld channels off the Scheldt mouth after pilot error and sank in sight of land; all ~256 aboard the two ships died.
cargo: Three chests of coin for the Indies trade: gold ducats and silver (much recovered, including some 2,000 gold ducats), plus wine, cheese and trade goods.
discovery: Found in 1981 using VOC archival charts; excavated 1981-2000s under Dutch supervision with Rex Cowan; finds shared between the Netherlands and salvors, many in Zeeland museums.
sources: Nationaal Archief, VOC records (Zeeland chamber)
Rex Cowan & J.H.G. Gawronski, De VOC schepen 't Vliegend Hart (publications on the 1981+ excavation)

name: Slot ter Hooge
story: Outward bound for Batavia with silver, she was driven onto the north coast of Porto Santo (Madeira) in a storm on 19 November 1724; 221 of 254 aboard died. The VOC contracted the celebrated Devon inventor John Lethbridge, whose leather 'diving engine' (a wooden barrel apparatus) recovered most of the silver in 1725-1734 — a landmark in diving history.
cargo: Nineteen chests of silver: bar silver and coin (~1.5 tons), much salvaged by Lethbridge; remaining bars recovered in the 20th century.
discovery: Relocated in 1974 by Rex Cowan using Lethbridge's own notes and VOC archives; silver bars and cannon raised; finds in Dutch and Madeiran collections.
sources: Rex Cowan, The Treasure of the 'Slot ter Hooge' / IJNA reports
Nationaal Archief VOC records; John Lethbridge correspondence

name: Akerendam
ship_type: VOC East Indiaman (~700 tons), maiden voyage
story: On her maiden voyage from Texel to Batavia in early 1725 she was blown far off course in a North Sea storm and wrecked on 8 March 1725 off the bird island of Runde, western Norway; all ~200 aboard perished.
cargo: Nineteen chests of gold and silver coin (~57,000 guilders in gold ducats and silver rijders) to fund VOC Asian trade.
discovery: Found in summer 1972 by Norwegian and Swedish sport divers; some 57,000 coins recovered — still Norway's richest treasure find ('Rundeskatten'); shares went to the finders, Norway and the Netherlands; coins displayed in Bergen and Alesund.
sources: Bergen Maritime Museum / Sunnmore Museum on the Runde treasure
Bruijn, Gaastra & Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping (voyage 'Akerendam' 1725)

name: Zuytdorp
story: Bound from the Cape to Batavia in 1712 with a coin cargo, she overshot the Brouwer-route turn north and struck the base of towering cliffs (now the Zuytdorp Cliffs) between the Murchison River and Shark Bay, Western Australia. Survivors reached the clifftop — relics and camps suggest some lived with, or died among, Aboriginal people; no survivor ever reached Batavia, and the VOC never learned her fate.
cargo: Newly minted 1711 silver schellingen and double stuivers from the Middelburg mint; a 'carpet of silver' was found in the surf zone.
discovery: Wreckage and coins spotted by stockman Tom Pepper in 1927; identified in 1958 (Phillip Playford); investigated by the Western Australian Museum from the 1970s; protected under the Commonwealth Historic Shipwrecks Act.
sources: Western Australian Museum, Zuytdorp wreck site records
Phillip Playford, Carpet of Silver: The Wreck of the Zuytdorp (1996)

name: Zeewijk
story: Running her easting down for Batavia, she struck Half Moon Reef in the Pelsaert Group of the Houtman Abrolhos off Western Australia on 9 June 1727. Survivors lived for months on the islands, and after a longboat sent for help vanished, built a small sloop — the Sloepie, the first European-style vessel built in Australia — from wreckage and mangrove wood, sailing 82 survivors and most of the money chests to Batavia in 1728.
cargo: Ten chests of silver coin (~315,800 guilders), most carried to Batavia in the Sloepie.
discovery: Wreck site identified in the 19th-20th centuries (guano workers, then systematic Western Australian Museum survey/excavation 1976-1981); large artifact collection in the WA Museum.
sources: Western Australian Museum, Zeewijk site reports
Hugh Edwards, Islands of Angry Ghosts (context); VOC daghregister of the survivors

name: Le Chameau
ship_type: French royal flute / transport (~600 tons, 48 guns)
story: The crown transport carrying the year's pay and funds for New France, plus incoming officials, was driven onto rocks (Chameau Rock, off Kelpy Cove near Louisbourg, Cape Breton) in a gale on the night of 26-27 August 1725; all ~310-316 aboard died, including the incoming intendant of New France. One of the worst disasters in French colonial Canada.
cargo: Approximately 290,000 livres in gold louis d'or and silver ecus for the garrison and government of New France, plus passengers' goods.
discovery: Treasure pocket located in 1965 by Alex Storm, David MacEachern and Harvey MacLeod after a systematic search; coins auctioned 1971 — Canada's most famous treasure-wreck recovery.
sources: Alex Storm, Canada's Treasure Hunt / Seaweed and Gold
Parks Canada and Nova Scotia Museum records on Le Chameau
French colonial archives (Archives nationales d'outre-mer) on the 1725 loss

name: HMS Association (Scilly naval disaster)
captain: Fleet: Admiral Sir Cloudesley Shovell; flag captain Edmund Loades
story: Returning from Toulon, Shovell's fleet fatally misjudged its longitude and ran onto the Western Rocks of the Isles of Scilly on the night of 22 October 1707. Association struck the Outer Gilstone and sank in minutes; Eagle, Romney and Firebrand were also lost. Between 1,400 and 2,000 men died, including Shovell — a catastrophe that helped drive the Longitude Act of 1714.
cargo: Fleet pay chests and Shovell's plate; quantities of coin recovered in modern salvage.
discovery: Found in 1967 by Royal Navy divers (Engledow/Roland Morris operations followed); thousands of coins and cannon raised 1967-70s; site now protected under the UK Protection of Wrecks Act.
sources: Historic England protected wreck records (Association, Gilstone Ledges)
W.E. May, 'The Last Voyage of Sir Clowdisley Shovel', Journal of Navigation; Dava Sobel, Longitude (1995)

name: Nuestra Senora de Guadalupe (with Conde de Tolosa)
captain: Francisco Barrero y Pelaez (fleet under Baltasar de Guevara)
story: Two royal azogues carrying mercury for the Mexican silver mints were caught by a hurricane on 24-25 August 1724 and driven into Samana Bay, Hispaniola. Guadalupe grounded on a sandbank and most of her ~550 people survived by wading and rafting ashore; Tolosa hung up on a reef and lost ~600, with a handful surviving over a month in her mizzen rigging before rescue.
cargo: Together ~400 tons of quicksilver (mercury) in leather bags inside chests, plus passengers' valuables and trade goods.
discovery: Found 1976-77 by Tracy Bowden under contract with the Dominican government; yielded superb collections of glassware, jewelry and everyday objects, displayed in the Museo de las Atarazanas Reales, Santo Domingo.
sources: Tracy Bowden, 'Gleaning Treasure from the Silver Bank' / National Geographic (1996 era reports on the azogues)
Museo de las Atarazanas Reales, Santo Domingo; AGI Seville records of the 1724 azogue fleet

name: HMS Feversham
story: Escorting transports with provisions and pay for the Quebec expedition's aftermath, Feversham and three transports (Joseph, Mary, Neptune) wrecked on Scatarie Island, Cape Breton, on 7 October 1711, weeks after the Walker expedition's own disaster in the St Lawrence; over 100 died.
cargo: Provisions and a quantity of colonial coin (New England and Spanish silver) — coins recovered in 1980s salvage are prized by numismatists as dated colonial treasure.
discovery: Located and salvaged in 1984 off Scatarie Island under Nova Scotia permit; coins (including Massachusetts silver) sold at auction; site documented by Nova Scotia authorities.
sources: Nova Scotia Museum / Maritime Museum of the Atlantic wreck databases
Numismatic literature on the 1711 Feversham colonial coin hoard (e.g., Stack's auction documentation)

name: De Liefde
story: Taking the northabout route around Scotland (to avoid Channel privateers in the War of the Spanish Succession), she smashed into Mio Ness in the Out Skerries, Shetland, in a November 1711 storm; of ~300 aboard, the sole survivor was the lookout thrown onto the rocks.
cargo: Money chests reported at 227,000 guilders in silver coin for the Indies trade.
discovery: Salvaged sporadically since the 18th century; relocated 1964-67 with major recoveries of silver ducatons by teams including Royal Navy divers; finds in Shetland Museum, Lerwick.
sources: Shetland Museum & Archives, De Liefde finds
Bruijn, Gaastra & Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping (De Liefde outward voyage 1711)

name: Anna Catharina
ship_type: VOC East Indiaman (smaller consort of 't Vliegend Hert)
captain: (master recorded in VOC Zeeland chamber lists)
story: Sailed in company with 't Vliegend Hert from Rammekens on 3 February 1735 and was lost the same night in the banks off the Scheldt mouth after the pilot's channel error; lost with all hands. Her loss is documented in the same VOC inquiry as the Vliegend Hert.
cargo: Outward VOC lading and specie for Batavia.
discovery: Unlike her consort, the Anna Catharina's wreck has not been positively identified/excavated; position is inferred from the 1735 inquiry charts.
sources: Nationaal Archief, VOC Zeeland chamber records of the 3 Feb 1735 double loss
Publications of the 't Vliegend Hert excavation (Cowan/Gawronski)

name: Mary Anne (Bellamy flotilla wine pink)
story: A Dublin-owned wine carrier taken by Bellamy's flotilla off Cape Cod hours before the 26 April 1717 storm; the drunken prize crew ran her aground near Pochet Island, Orleans, the same night the Whydah sank. The seven pirates aboard survived, were captured, tried in Boston, and six were hanged in November 1717 — their trial record is a key primary source on Bellamy's crew.
cargo: Madeira wine.
discovery: Not located; position from the 1717 trial depositions describing the stranding at Pochet, Orleans (Cape Cod).
sources: The Trials of Eight Persons Indited for Piracy (Boston, 1717)
Colin Woodard, The Republic of Pirates (2007)
